docs.title: Home
docs.text:
supply chain security · python · zero config Block the attack before it lands.

pipguard scans every package via AST analysis before code runs. No database. No network calls. No configuration. Just a guard at the door.

Zero config Pure stdlib Blocks before install CI-ready pip install pipguard Read the docs → bash — pipguard $pipguard install litellm==1.82.8 → Downloading wheel (no code executed) → Extracting archive → AST scanning 47 files   setup.py          ............... CLEAN   utils/loader.py  ............... CLEAN   .pth files       ............... CRITICAL   ─────────────────────────────────────   ✗ BLOCKED: litellm==1.82.8   .pth autorun · reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa   exfiltrates to 44.202.x.x:4444   Severity: CRITICAL · Exit code: 1

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
The March 2026 litellm attack (97M downloads/month) embedded Python code in a .pth file — executed automatically at interpreter startup, exfiltrating SSH keys, AWS credentials, and Kubernetes configs from a single pip install.

Classical tools (pip-audit, GuardDog) are blind to zero-day attacks. They check known signatures. pipguard asks a different question:

The question classical tools never ask

Should any pip install be allowed to read ~/.ssh/id_rsa?

The answer is no. And that question doesn't require a database.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: 01 pip download Downloads wheel or sdist.No code runs. Ever. 02 Sdist check Exit 2 if sdist detected — build scripts are unsafe. 03 Extract zipfile/tarfile only.No subprocess. No exec. 04 AST scan Parallel scan of all .py files. CRITICAL scope on .pth, setup.py. 05 Risk score CRITICAL/HIGH → exit 1.CLEAN → install silently.

docs.title: Risk Levels at a Glance
docs.text:
Level What triggers it Action CRITICAL .pth executable code; eval(base64.b64decode(...)) Block (exit 1) HIGH Non-ASCII package name (homoglyph); reads ~/.ssh, ~/.aws in install hooks; shell=True; os.system() Block (exit 1) MEDIUM Binary-only wheel; network in runtime; sensitive env vars Warn + confirm LOW Compiled binary extension in mixed wheel; dynamic imports Warn + confirm CLEAN None of the above Install silently

Full risk level reference →

docs.title: Seed Allowlist
docs.text:
Some packages legitimately access credential stores or perform sensitive operations as part of their core purpose. pipguard ships with a seed allowlist that reduces their finding from HIGH to MEDIUM so they don't cause false-positive blocks.

CRITICAL is never reduced

The seed allowlist (and --allow) only reduces HIGH → MEDIUM. CRITICAL findings always block, regardless of any allowlist entry.

docs.title: Built-in Allowlist
docs.text: Package Why it accesses credentials keyring Credential storage library — reads/writes system keyring by design keyrings.alt Alternative keyring backends boto3 AWS SDK — reads ~/.aws/credentials for authentication botocore AWS core library (boto3 dependency) awscli AWS CLI — reads ~/.aws/config paramiko SSH client — reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa for key-based auth google-auth Google authentication — reads ~/.config/gcloud/ google-cloud-storage Google Cloud SDK google-cloud-bigquery Google Cloud BigQuery google-cloud-core Google Cloud base library azure-identity Azure authentication library

docs.title: Per-Invocation Allowlist
docs.text:
Add packages to the allowlist for a single invocation with --allow:

pipguard install --allow my-internal-auth-lib -r requirements.txt

Multiple packages:

pipguard install --allow pkg-a --allow pkg-b -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Adding to the Project Allowlist
docs.text: To suggest adding a package to the built-in seed allowlist, open an issue at github.com/shenxianpeng/pipguard with evidence of why the credential access is legitimate.

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
pipguard uses structured exit codes so CI pipelines can react precisely.

Code Meaning When it occurs 0 Clean — install succeeded All packages scanned as CLEAN (or MEDIUM/LOW with user confirmation) 1 Blocked — CRITICAL or HIGH risk detected Any package triggered a CRITICAL or HIGH finding 2 Scan error — could not complete the scan Download failed, unsupported archive format, disk space error

docs.title: Usage in CI
docs.text:
pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt
echo "Exit code: $?"
GitHub Actions
- name: Secure dependency install
  run: pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt
  # Step fails automatically on exit 1 or 2
GitLab CI
install:
  script:
    - pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Distinguishing Exit Codes
docs.text:
If you need to handle scan errors differently from blocked packages:

pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt
EXIT=$?
if [ $EXIT -eq 1 ]; then
  echo "Security block — malicious package detected"
  exit 1
elif [ $EXIT -eq 2 ]; then
  echo "Scan error — check network or archive format"
  exit 2
fi

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: pipguard's design has one rule: code must never execute during scanning.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
pipguard install X
       │
       ▼
pip download --prefer-binary X    ← downloads wheel/sdist, no code execution
       │
       ▼
Detect sdist fallback             ← exit 2 if sdist detected (unless --allow-sdist)
       │
       ▼
Extract archive (zipfile/tarfile) ← never executes code
       │
       ▼
AST scan all .py files            ← parallel, ThreadPoolExecutor
  setup.py, pyproject.toml, *.pth ← CRITICAL/HIGH scope
  all other .py                   ← MEDIUM/LOW scope
       │
       ▼
Risk scoring:
  CRITICAL → block (exit 1)
  HIGH     → block (exit 1)
  MEDIUM   → warn + confirm
  LOW      → warn + confirm
  CLEAN    → install silently
       │
       ▼
pip install --no-index            ← installs FROM SCANNED FILES (TOCTOU-safe)
    --find-links /tmp/pipguard-XX

docs.title: Why Pre-Install?
docs.text:
Classical security tools (pip-audit, Safety, GuardDog) work post-hoc — they check installed packages against known-bad signature databases. This means:

  1. Zero-day blind spot — a new attack not yet in the database walks straight through
  2. Race condition — the malicious code has already run by the time the tool checks

pipguard reverses the order. It asks: does this code do something that any pip install should be allowed to do?

Regardless of whether the package is on any watchlist, the answer to "reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa and sends it over a network" is always no.

docs.title: TOCTOU Safety
docs.text:
A subtle attack vector: scan a clean file, then swap it for a malicious one before install.

pipguard counters this by:

  1. Downloading the archive to a temp directory
  2. Scanning the files in place in that temp directory
  3. Running pip install --no-index --find-links /tmp/pipguard-XX — installing the exact files that were scanned

The archive is never re-downloaded or re-extracted after scanning.

docs.title: AST Scanning
docs.text: pipguard uses Python's built-in ast module — no third-party dependencies — to parse .py files into abstract syntax trees and walk the nodes looking for dangerous patterns.

docs.title: What gets flagged
docs.text:
CRITICALHIGHMEDIUMLOW Pattern Example .pth file with executable Python import os; os.system(...) in .pth Obfuscated eval eval(base64.b64decode(...)) Network in setup.py / install hooks urllib.request.urlopen(...) in setup.py Shell/subprocess execution in install hooks os.system(...), subprocess.run(..., shell=True) Pattern Example Non-ASCII character in package name bоto3 with Cyrillic о — possible homoglyph/typosquatting attack Credential path read in install hooks open('~/.ssh/id_rsa') in setup.py Subprocess execution in install hooks subprocess.run([...]) Pattern Example Binary-only wheel (no Python source) Wheel with only .so / .pyd / .dylib files Network in runtime code urllib.request.urlopen(...) in utils.py Sensitive env var access os.environ.get('AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY') Large source file over 1MB scanner emits confidence-reduction warning Binary IOC string hit .so contains https://... or /bin/sh Pattern Example Compiled binary extension in mixed wheel .so / .pyd / .dylib alongside .py source Dynamic imports importlib.import_module(name) __import__() __import__(variable)

Binary files are also scanned with lightweight IOC string matching (first 2MB) to surface obvious credential-path or exfiltration indicators.

docs.title: Homoglyph / Typosquatting Detection
docs.text:
Before scanning archive contents, pipguard checks the package name itself for non-ASCII characters (e.g. Cyrillic о substituted for Latin o). Any such character produces a HIGH finding regardless of what the package contains:

bоto3   ← Cyrillic 'о' (U+043E) in position 1
↑ visually identical to boto3, but a different string

Package names are also NFKC-normalized before allowlist comparison, so a homoglyph name cannot bypass the allowlist by mimicking a trusted package.

docs.title: Binary Extension Scanning
docs.text:
pipguard detects compiled binary extension files (.so, .pyd, .dylib) in extracted wheels. Static AST scanning cannot inspect these files, so pipguard flags them explicitly:

  • Mixed wheel (.py source + binary extensions): each extension file generates a LOW finding — the scanner covered the Python parts but is blind to any payload in compiled code.
  • Binary-only wheel (no .py source at all): a single MEDIUM finding is emitted, and the confirmation gate fires. pipguard's core scan promise cannot be fulfilled for packages with no Python source.

docs.title: Seed Allowlist
docs.text:
Some packages legitimately access credentials as part of their core purpose. pipguard ships with a seed allowlist that reduces their finding from HIGH to MEDIUM (CRITICAL is never reduced):

keyring, keyrings.alt, boto3, botocore, awscli, paramiko, google-auth, google-cloud-storage, google-cloud-bigquery, google-cloud-core, azure-identity

Full allowlist reference →

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Phase 1 scope

These are known limitations of the current static-analysis approach.

  • Obfuscation — multi-layer obfuscation (e.g. exec(compile(...)) wrapped multiple times) may evade detection
  • C extensions — .so / .pyd binaries are opaque to AST scanning; flagged as LOW (mixed) or MEDIUM (binary-only) to surface the blind spot
  • Python/pip only — no npm, cargo, or go module support
  • Phase 2 (in design) — seccomp/eBPF sandbox for capability-level interception at runtime

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • pip (any recent version)
  • Zero external dependencies — pipguard uses only the Python standard library

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text:
pip install pipguard

Verify the installation:

pipguard --version

docs.title: Install from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/shenxianpeng/pipguard.git
cd pipguard
pip install -e .

docs.title: CI / Docker
docs.text:
Add to your requirements-dev.txt or install directly in your CI pipeline:

.github/workflows/ci.yml
- name: Install pipguard
  run: pip install pipguard

- name: Secure dependency install
  run: pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text: pip install --upgrade pipguard

docs.title: Uninstalling
docs.text:
pip uninstall pipguard

pipguard creates no configuration files and leaves no persistent state — uninstall is clean.

docs.title: Policy as Code
docs.text:
pipguard can load an optional policy file to enforce organization-wide defaults.

Default path:

./pipguard.toml

Or pass explicitly:

pipguard install --policy ./security/pipguard.toml -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
[install]
require_hashes = true
allow_vcs_pinned = true
allow_direct_url_pinned = true
binary_only = "block"  # prompt | block | allow

[allowlist]
seed = ["my-internal-auth-lib", "corp-keyring"]

[intel]
feed = "https://example.org/pipguard-feed.json"
enforce = true

docs.title: Keys
docs.text:
• require_hashes (bool): enforce hash-locked dependencies.
  • allow_vcs_pinned (bool): allow VCS requirements only when pinned to commit hash.
  • allow_direct_url_pinned (bool): allow direct URL dependencies only with hash fragment.
  • binary_only (string): behavior when binary-only wheel is detected:
  • prompt (default): keep MEDIUM behavior.
  • block: fail installation (exit 1).
  • allow: do not enforce additional binary-only policy block.

[intel] section:

  • feed (string): local file path or HTTPS URL to a JSON denylist feed.
  • enforce (bool): if true, block packages in the feed before scan/install.

[allowlist] section:

  • seed (list[string]): project-level seed allowlist entries. These are merged with the built-in seed allowlist and --allow CLI flags.

docs.title: Risk Levels
docs.text: pipguard assigns every scanned package one of five risk levels based on patterns found during static AST analysis.

docs.title: CRITICAL
docs.text:
Action: Block immediately. Exit 1. No confirmation prompt.

Trigger Why it's CRITICAL .pth file containing executable Python code .pth files are executed automatically at Python interpreter startup — before any user code runs. Any executable content is unambiguously malicious. eval(base64.b64decode(...)) in any file Classic obfuscated payload pattern. Legitimate packages never need this. Network calls (urllib, httpx, requests, socket) in setup.py or install hooks Build-time network calls have no legitimate use case. The March 2026 litellm attack used this to exfiltrate data. Shell/subprocess execution in install hooks (os.system, os.popen, shell execution via subprocess) Install-time command execution is a high-confidence attacker primitive and is blocked by default.

CRITICAL is never reduced

The --allow flag and the seed allowlist do not reduce CRITICAL findings. Only --force bypasses CRITICAL — use with extreme caution.

docs.title: HIGH
docs.text:
Action: Block. Exit 1. No confirmation prompt.

Trigger Scope Why Non-ASCII character in package name Name check (pre-scan) Possible homoglyph / typosquatting attack (e.g. bоto3 with Cyrillic о instead of Latin o). Reads credential paths (~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.kube, ~/.gnupg) Install hooks only A package reading your SSH keys during pip install is an attack, not a feature. Direct subprocess execution (non-shell) Install hooks only Elevated risk in install-time context; surfaced as HIGH for manual review. Binary IOC credential markers (/.ssh/id_rsa, /.aws/credentials) Binary extension scan Heuristic binary content includes hard-coded credential path indicators.

HIGH in runtime code

If the same patterns appear in runtime code (not install hooks), the finding is downgraded to MEDIUM. boto3 legitimately reads ~/.aws at runtime — that's fine. It reading ~/.aws in setup.py is not.

docs.title: MEDIUM
docs.text:
Action: Warn and prompt for confirmation. (--yes skips prompt and proceeds.)

Trigger Notes Binary-only wheel (no Python source) Wheel contains only .so / .pyd / .dylib files — AST scan cannot verify contents. Confirmation gate fires; use --yes to proceed. Network calls in runtime .py files Common in legitimate packages; shown for transparency Sensitive env var access (*TOKEN*, *KEY*, *SECRET*, *PASSWORD*, *CREDENTIAL*) Flagged in runtime code Large source file over 1MB Scanner continues, but emits confidence-reduction warning for manual review Binary IOC string indicators (https://, /bin/sh, socket) Heuristic binary scan detected suspicious runtime/exfil primitives

docs.title: LOW
docs.text:
Action: Warn (shown in summary). Confirmation prompt fires (skippable with --yes).

Trigger Notes Compiled binary extension in mixed wheel .so / .pyd / .dylib file present alongside Python source — AST scanner is blind to any payload in compiled code importlib.import_module(variable) Dynamic imports can load arbitrary code __import__(variable) Same concern as above

docs.title: CLEAN
docs.text:
Action: Install silently.

No patterns matching CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, or LOW were found. The package installs without any output.

docs.title: pipguard 现状评估与改进建议（功能与检查能力）
docs.text: 评估时间：2026-04-13 评估范围：当前仓库主流程（download → extract → AST scan → gated install）

docs.title: 1) 当前做得好的地方（功能层）
docs.text:
1. 默认阻断安装期高危行为：
  2. 对 install-hook 范围（setup.py/pyproject.toml/.pth）的网络调用、动态执行、shell 执行等行为设置为 HIGH/CRITICAL，默认阻断。
  3. 对 .pth autorun 攻击有针对性防护：
  4. 单独扫描 .pth，检测可执行语句，直接 CRITICAL。
  5. 具备 TOCTOU 防护思路：
  6. 安装阶段使用 pip install --no-index --find-links 从“已扫描目录”安装，避免二次拉取被替换。
  7. 对二进制盲区有显式提示：
  8. 二进制扩展（.so/.pyd/.dylib）会被标记为 LOW；纯二进制轮子会触发 MEDIUM。
  9. 工程质量基础较稳：
  10. 当前测试通过率高（187 passed, 1 skipped），说明已有行为在现有规则下较稳定。

docs.title: 2.1 可被绕过的“代码语义”攻击
docs.text:
1. 别名/包装调用绕过（高优先级）

  2. 当前网络检测依赖固定函数名（如 requests.get / urllib.request.urlopen）， 若攻击者写成：

    • import requests as r; r.get(...)
    • fn = requests.get; fn(...)
    • getattr(requests, "get")(... ) 很可能漏报。

  3. 反射与动态属性访问绕过

  4. 如 getattr(os, "system")("...")、__import__("socket") 后再动态调用。

  5. 目前主要覆盖直接调用路径，反射链可规避规则。

  6. 敏感路径检测偏字面量匹配

  7. 目前对凭据路径更多基于字符串常量包含关系， 对 os.path.join(os.path.expanduser("~"), ".ssh", key_name) 这类组合构造不够强。

  8. 命令执行检测范围有限

  9. 已覆盖 os.system/os.popen、subprocess.*(shell=True)，

  10. 但对 subprocess.Popen(["bash","-c", ...])（shell=False）或 pty/ctypes 触发执行，覆盖不足。

  11. 大文件跳过策略可被利用

  12. 单文件 >1MB 直接跳过 AST 扫描，攻击者可将恶意逻辑藏入超大脚本文件实现规避。

docs.title: 2.2 包管理与供应链元数据攻击面
docs.text:
1. requirements 生态支持面偏窄

  2. 当前对 VCS/直接 URL/本地路径依赖基本是“不支持即退出”， 在真实企业依赖结构中常见，导致工具“用不起来”，进而被绕过（团队直接不用 pipguard）。

  3. 缺少完整性校验闭环

  4. 当前核心是“行为扫描”，但缺失：
    • 强制 hash-pinning（--require-hashes 级别）
    • 与锁文件/制品仓库的一致性验证

  5. 这会让“同名不同内容”的供应链攻击仍有操作空间。

  6. 对 wheel 内元数据和构建后门检查不足

  7. 如 entry_points, console_scripts, .dist-info 元数据滥用、 或安装后触发链（首次 import 执行）缺乏专项规则。

docs.title: 2.3 二进制与原生扩展盲区
docs.text:
1. 二进制代码不可见（项目已识别但未真正解决）
  2. 目前只是“提示风险”，无法分析 C/Rust 扩展真实行为。
  3. 纯二进制包仅 MEDIUM
  4. 对高安全场景（CI/CD、生产镜像构建）不够严格，建议可配置为默认阻断。

docs.title: 2.4 运行时攻击能力未覆盖
docs.text:
1. 仅静态扫描，缺少行为沙箱
  2. 无法捕捉“静态看起来安全，但运行期解密/下载二阶段 payload”的攻击。
  3. 缺少系统调用/网络/DNS 实时拦截
  4. 不能在安装进程中基于能力策略阻断如 connect/open/read ~/.ssh 等实际操作。

docs.title: 3) 典型“仍可能防不住”的 attack 列表
docs.text:
按现实风险优先级排序：

  1. Alias + 反射混淆 exfiltration

  2. 使用别名/getattr/变量间接调用网络与文件读取，绕过函数名匹配。

  3. 大型单文件载荷（>1MB）隐藏恶意逻辑

  4. 利用扫描上限跳过解析。

  5. 纯二进制 wheel 内置恶意逻辑

  6. 通过 native 扩展直接执行系统调用/网络发送。

  7. 安装后延迟触发（post-install / first-import）

  8. 不在 install hook 里做坏事，而在首次 import 时执行。

  9. 利用合法能力组合达成恶意目标

  10. 比如不直接访问敏感路径，而先调用系统工具间接读取并 exfiltrate。

  11. 多层字符串拼接 + 解码 + exec 链

  12. 超出当前 b64 + exec 的单层模式规则。

docs.title: 阶段 A（1~2 周，可快速提升检出率）
docs.text:
1. 引入“简单数据流/别名追踪”
  2. 在 AST 层维护轻量符号表：
    • 跟踪 import x as y
    • 跟踪 fn = requests.get 这类一跳赋值

  3. 将调用点还原到 canonical API，再匹配规则。

  4. 扩展危险调用规则库

  5. 增补 getattr(..., "system")、Popen(["bash","-c"])、shlex/pty/ctypes 常见链路。

  6. 优化大文件策略

  7. 不再“直接跳过”，改成：

    • 分块抽样 + 关键 token 扫描
    • 并给出 MEDIUM/HIGH “未完全扫描”告警。

  8. 提高 install-hook 严格性（默认 deny）

  9. install-hook 里除白名单操作外，网络/命令执行默认 CRITICAL。

docs.title: 阶段 B（2~6 周，构建企业可用能力）
docs.text:
1. 增强供应链完整性功能
  2. 支持 requirements hash 校验模式；

  3. 生成并验证 lock 清单（包名+版本+hash）。

  4. 引入可配置策略文件（policy-as-code）

  5. 例如 pipguard.toml：

    • CI 中 binary_only = block
    • 允许域名白名单
    • 禁止 install-hook 网络。

  6. 支持更多依赖来源但保持安全默认

  7. VCS/URL 依赖可“受限支持”：必须 pin commit/hash，否则阻断。

docs.title: 阶段 C（中期方向，能力跃迁）
docs.text:
1. 运行时沙箱（seccomp/eBPF/容器隔离）

  2. 将安装过程放入隔离环境，按能力策略拦截：

    • 文件读（~/.ssh, ~/.aws）
    • 网络连接（任意外连或非白名单域名）
    • 子进程执行。

  3. 二进制扩展强化审计

  4. 对 .so/.pyd 做静态特征扫描（导入符号、可疑字符串、网络 API 引用）；
  5. 与签名可信源（内部仓库）结合。

docs.title: 5) 建议的目标指标（便于衡量改进效果）
docs.text:
1. 检测能力指标
  2. 恶意样本集检出率（TPR）

  3. 绕过样本集检出率（Alias/Reflection/Obfuscation）

  4. 误报指标

  5. Top 100 常用包误报率（FPR）

  6. 允许列表依赖比例（越低越好）

  7. 工程指标

  8. 扫描耗时（P50/P95）
  9. CI 接入成功率（项目可落地率）

docs.title: 6) 总结（给当前项目的判断）
docs.text:
• 定位判断：pipguard 目前是一个“方向正确、基础扎实”的 Phase-1 静态防线， 在防范“明显安装期恶意行为”方面有实际价值。
  • 核心短板：对“语义绕过、二进制 payload、运行时动态行为”防护不足。
  • 优先级建议：先做 别名/数据流增强 + 大文件策略 + policy 化， 再推进运行时沙箱，能在不大幅牺牲性能的情况下显著提升真实防护强度。

docs.title: Install a single package
docs.text:
pipguard install requests

pipguard will download, scan, and — if clean — install the package. No output means no findings.

docs.title: Install from requirements.txt
docs.text:
pipguard install -r requirements.txt

Scans all packages in the file. Blocks on first CRITICAL or HIGH finding.

docs.title: CI Mode
docs.text:
In CI, you never want interactive prompts. Use --yes to suppress all confirmation prompts and have pipguard exit 1 automatically on CRITICAL or HIGH findings:

pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt
GitHub Actions $pipguard install --yes -r requirements.txt ✓ requests==2.31.0 ✓ numpy==1.26.3 ✗ litellm==1.82.8 CRITICAL — .pth autorun, reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa Process exited with code 1

docs.title: Allowing Known-Legitimate Packages
docs.text:
Some packages legitimately access credential stores (e.g. paramiko reads ~/.ssh). Use --allow to reduce their finding from HIGH to MEDIUM:

pipguard install --allow paramiko paramiko

CRITICAL findings are never reduced

--allow only reduces HIGH → MEDIUM. CRITICAL findings always block, regardless of flags.

docs.title: Forcing a Package (Escape Hatch)
docs.text:
For known false-positives on fully-trusted internal packages:

pipguard install --force my-trusted-internal-pkg

Use with care

--force bypasses all checks and logs a warning. Never use in CI without code review.

docs.title: Allowing sdist Packages
docs.text:
By default pipguard exits with code 2 if a package falls back to sdist (source distribution), because sdists execute build scripts. To opt in:

pipguard install --allow-sdist some-package

sdist installs execute arbitrary code

--allow-sdist bypasses a hard safety boundary. Even though pipguard runs AST scanning on setup.py, pip install will still execute setup.py and any build-backend code at install time. pipguard's AST scan does NOT prevent this. Never use --allow-sdist in automated pipelines without explicit review.

docs.title: All Flags
docs.text: Flag Description -r FILE Install from requirements file --yes / -y CI mode — no prompts, exit 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH --allow PKG Add package to per-invocation allowlist (HIGH→MEDIUM) --force PKG Bypass all checks for a specific package --allow-sdist Allow sdist fallback (DANGER: executes arbitrary code — AST scan does NOT prevent this) --require-hashes Require hash-locked requirements entries (--hash=... or URL hash fragment) --policy FILE Load policy file (default: ./pipguard.toml if present) --intel-feed FILE_OR_URL Threat-intel JSON feed containing blocked package versions --enforce-intel Enforce intel feed denylist and block matching packages

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Clean — all packages installed 1 Blocked — CRITICAL or HIGH risk detected 2 Scan error — download failed or unsupported format

hide: - navigation - toc

supply chain security · python · zero config Block the attack before it lands.

pipguard scans every package via AST analysis before code runs. No database. No network calls. No configuration. Just a guard at the door.

Zero config Pure stdlib Blocks before install CI-ready pip install pipguard Read the docs → bash — pipguard $pipguard install litellm==1.82.8 → Downloading wheel (no code executed) → Extracting archive → AST scanning 47 files   setup.py          ............... CLEAN   utils/loader.py  ............... CLEAN   .pth files       ............... CRITICAL   ─────────────────────────────────────   ✗ BLOCKED: litellm==1.82.8   .pth autorun · reads ~/.ssh/id_rsa   exfiltrates to 44.202.x.x:4444   Severity: CRITICAL · Exit code: 1

docs.title: Seed Allowlist
docs.text: 本页面提供中文入口，内容将持续完善。

docs.title: Seed Allowlist
docs.text:
Some packages legitimately access credential stores or perform sensitive operations as part of their core purpose. pipguard ships with a seed allowlist that reduces their finding from HIGH to MEDIUM so they don't cause false-positive blocks.

CRITICAL is never reduced

The seed allowlist (and --allow) only reduces HIGH → MEDIUM. CRITICAL findings always block, regardless of any allowlist entry.

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text: 本页面提供中文入口，内容将持续完善。

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
pipguard uses structured exit codes so CI pipelines can react precisely.

Code Meaning When it occurs 0 Clean — install succeeded All packages scanned as CLEAN (or MEDIUM/LOW with user confirmation) 1 Blocked — CRITICAL or HIGH risk detected Any package triggered a CRITICAL or HIGH finding 2 Scan error — could not complete the scan Download failed, unsupported archive format, disk space error

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: pipguard's design has one rule: code must never execute during scanning.

docs.title: Policy as Code
docs.text:
pipguard can load an optional policy file to enforce organization-wide defaults.

Default path:

./pipguard.toml

Or pass explicitly:

pipguard install --policy ./security/pipguard.toml -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Risk Levels
docs.text: 本页面提供中文入口，内容将持续完善。

docs.title: Risk Levels
docs.text: pipguard assigns every scanned package one of five risk levels based on patterns found during static AST analysis.
